conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility properly, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now let me start by asking you some basic questions to assess your eligibility.

Do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 and still in sixth form doing his A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know about Alex.

Since Alex is 17 and in sixth form doing his A-levels, that would typically count as approved education. Let me ask you a few more questions to fully assess your eligibility.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex and your living arrangements.

Do you live with Alex, or if not, do you contribute financially towards his upkeep (such as for food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One more question to ensure I have all the necessary information:

Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he claiming any benefits in his own right such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't started working 24 hours a week or anything like that, and he's not claiming any benefits himself.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me and the official guidance, I can now provide you with a determination.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- Alex is 17 and studying A-levels in sixth form, which counts as approved education
- You can claim Child Benefit for children under 20 if they stay in approved education or training
- You live in the UK and Alex lives at home with you, making you responsible for him
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex hasn't started working 24+ hours per week or claiming benefits in his own right

You should be able to make a claim for Child Benefit. Please note: if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is in sixth form doing his A-levels, which qualifies as approved education. According to the eligibility rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. You live in the UK (Manchester), Alex lives at home with you, which means you are responsible for him. Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex has not started paid work for 24 hours or more per week and is not claiming benefits in his own right such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old son Alex who is in approved education (sixth form A-levels). They are responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming for him, and Alex has not started full-time work or claimed benefits in his own right. All eligibility criteria are met.

Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
